title: Convertible Desk-to-Table
summary: A compact desk with a drawer that hinges to a twin, folding open into a full dining table — designed for a tiny house but useful in any small space.
description:
Built for a tiny house on wheels, this is a standalone desk with a drawer that becomes half of a dining table when two of them hinge together. Space that has to do double duty is the whole idea, and the mechanism is refreshingly simple: no sliding hardware, just hinges along the back legs of two matching desks so they fold face-to-face and open into a table.

The joinery is pocket-hole throughout, with a sensible rule of thumb: use 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws whenever 3/4" material is involved, and 1-1/2" holes with 2-1/2" screws when you're joining two 1-1/2" pieces. The aprons inset 1/4" from the outside faces, which gives the desk a crisp shadow line and leaves room for the drawer face.

The one detail that makes the conversion work: if you're building the hinged pair, the tabletop must not overhang on the back and side edges that hinge to the second desk, or the two won't close flush. Plan the top's overhang deliberately. And because it's a tiny-house piece, the legs can be shortened to clear a wheel well — check your specific space before cutting them to length.

tools.note: Whole build. Two settings — 3/4" for 1-by, 1-1/2" for 2x2 joints.
A circular saw handles the square crosscuts.

materials.name: Tabletop, min 19-1/4" x 51-1/2"
materials.note: Project panel or plywood with one smooth face.

materials.name: Hinges
materials.note: Only if building the convertible pair — along the back legs.

cutList.note: Shorten to clear a wheel well if needed.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut four legs at 29-1/4", two bottom side supports at 15-3/4" and one bottom support at 48" from the 2x2. Cut two top side supports at 15-3/4" and two top front/back supports at 48" from the 1x2. Cut one back apron at 48", two side aprons at 15-3/4", two front aprons at 12" and two drawer supports at 16-3/4" from the 1x4. Cut two drawer sides at 16" and two drawer front/back at 21-1/2" from the 1x3. The 1/4" plywood is used whole as the 16" x 23" drawer bottom. Rip the drawer face to 3" wide and cut it 23-3/4" long from a 1x4 offcut, and cut the tabletop to 51-1/2" x 19-1/4". Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to three 8 ft 2x2, one 12 ft 1x2, two 8 ft 1x4 and one 8 ft 1x3 — the drawer face packs onto the same two 1x4 boards as the aprons, so no third 1x4 is needed. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw; several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the two leg sets
steps.body: Each leg set is two 29-1/4" legs joined by one 15-3/4" 2x2 bottom side support low down and one 15-3/4" 1x2 top side support flush with the leg tops, which makes each finished set 18-3/4" across the outside faces of its legs. Use the plan's two-setting rule: 1-1/2" pocket holes with 2-1/2" screws where the 2x2 bottom support meets the 2x2 legs, and 3/4" holes with 1-1/4" screws for the 3/4"-thick 1x2 top support. Clamp both sets on the same flat surface and mark the bottom support's height across all four legs at once — the source does not name that height, so it is your call, but if the two sets disagree nothing you build on top of them will sit level. Glue every joint and check each set across the diagonals before the glue sets.

steps.title: Connect the leg sets
steps.body: Stand the two leg sets 48" apart and join them with the two 48" 1x2 top front/back supports, one at the front and one at the back, flush with the leg tops. Those supports fit between the legs, so the finished frame is 51" long over the legs and 18-3/4" deep — the numbers the 51-1/2" x 19-1/4" tabletop is sized against, giving a 1/4" overhang all round. There is only one 48" 2x2 bottom support in the cut list, so it can only go on one face; put it at the back, level with the 2x2 bottom side supports, and join it with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws. Measure both diagonals across the top of the frame and rack it until they agree before the glue grabs, because every apron from here on references this rectangle.

steps.title: Add the aprons
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends of all five 1x4 aprons and along their top edges as well — the top-edge holes are what you will screw the tabletop down through later, and they are far easier to drill now than upside down under a finished desk. Fit the 48" back apron between the back legs, the two 15-3/4" side aprons between front and back legs, and the two 12" front aprons at the front, one at each end; 48" less two 12" aprons leaves a 24" clear opening for the drawer. Fasten everything with 1-1/4" pocket screws and glue — 3/4" screws will not hold in 3/4" stock — insetting each apron 1/4" from the outside faces of the legs for the shadow line the design depends on.

steps.title: Fit the drawer supports
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the ends and along the top edges of the two 16-3/4" drawer supports, then fasten one inside each end of the drawer opening, running front to back behind the 12" front aprons, with 1-1/4" screws and glue. These are the vertical faces the 16" Euro slides screw to, so set them at exactly the same height on both sides — measure both down from the top of the aprons rather than up from the floor, and a slide that ends up 1/8" out of parallel with its mate will bind. Their inside faces need to finish 24" apart to match the opening, which is what gives the 23" drawer box its 1/2" of slide clearance per side.

steps.title: Build the drawer and face
steps.body: Build the drawer box with the two 16" sides running the full front-to-back depth and the two 21-1/2" front and back pieces fitting between them; that makes the box 23" wide outside, which is exactly what the 16" x 23" plywood bottom is cut to. Join the corners with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" screws and glue, then glue and brad-nail the plywood bottom onto the underside of the box, using the sheet itself to square the box as you nail. Mount the 16" Euro slides bottom-corner style and hang the box — a 23" box in the 24" opening is exactly the 1/2" per side these slides want. Finally hold the 23-3/4" x 3" drawer face on with a 1/8" gap down each side of the 24" opening and along its bottom edge, leaving the larger gap at the top where the tabletop lip hides it; tack it with two brads, pull the drawer out, and fix it permanently with 1-1/4" screws driven from inside the box.

steps.title: Attach the top and add hinges
steps.body: Remove the drawer, lay the 51-1/2" x 19-1/4" tabletop face down on a blanket and set the frame upside down on it. For a single desk, centre the frame so the top overhangs 1/4" on all four sides. If you are building the convertible pair, slide the frame instead so the top finishes flush with the back edge and with the side that will hinge, and put the whole 1/2" of overhang on the other two edges — a top that projects past the hinge line stops the two desks closing face to face, which is the one detail that makes the conversion work. Drive 1-1/4" screws up through the apron and 1x2 support pocket holes into the underside of the top, with a little glue, checking square as you go. Stand it up, refit the drawer, sand everything and ease the edges, then mount the two hinges along the back legs so the pair folds open into one table.

images.alt: Compact desk with a drawer that hinges open into a dining table
